content:
# Numbers Chapter 14 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 164-166 | Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0122-0123 (LXX)
**Source Images:** `NUM_014_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_014_page_002.jpg`, `NUM_014_page_003.jpg`; `NUM_014_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_014_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

### The People Weep and Rebel (vv. 1-4)

**1** And the whole congregation lifted up their voice and cried out, and the people wept that night.

**2** And all the Sons of Israel murmured against Moses and against Aaron, and the whole congregation said to them, "Would that we had died in the land of Egypt, or would that we had died in this wilderness!

**3** And why is YHWH bringing us to this land, to fall by the sword? Our wives and our little ones will become plunder. Would it not be better for us to return to Egypt?"

**4** And they said, each man to his brother, "Let us appoint a head and let us return to Egypt."

---

### Moses, Aaron, Joshua, and Caleb Respond (vv. 5-10)

**5** And Moses and Aaron fell on their faces before all the assembly of the congregation of the Sons of Israel.

**6** And Joshua son of Nun and Caleb son of Jephunneh, who were among those who had spied out the land, tore their garments.

**7** And they spoke to all the congregation of the Sons of Israel, saying, "The land through which we passed to spy it out — the land is exceedingly good, very much so.

**8** If YHWH delights in us, then he will bring us into this land and give it to us, a land which flows with milk and honey.

**9** Only do not rebel against YHWH, and do not fear the people of the land, for they are our bread. Their shade has departed from upon them, and YHWH is with us. Do not fear them."

**10** And the whole congregation said to stone them with stones. And the glory of YHWH appeared at the tent of meeting to all the Sons of Israel.

---

### YHWH's Wrath and Moses' Intercession (vv. 11-19)

**11** And YHWH said to Moses, "How long will this people despise me? And how long will they not believe in me, with all the signs which I have performed in their midst?

**12** I will strike them with pestilence and dispossess them, and I will make you into a nation greater and mightier than they."

**13** And Moses said to YHWH, "Then the Egyptians will hear — for you brought up this people in your might from their midst —

**14** and they will tell it to the inhabitants of this land. They have heard that you, YHWH, are in the midst of this people, that you, YHWH, are seen eye to eye, and your cloud stands over them, and in a pillar of cloud you go before them by day and in a pillar of fire by night.

**15** And if you put this people to death as one man, then the nations who have heard the report of you will say,

**16** 'Because YHWH was not able to bring this people into the land which he swore to them, he slaughtered them in the wilderness.'

**17** And now, let the power of my Lord be great, just as you have spoken, saying,

**18** 'YHWH is slow to anger and great in steadfast love, forgiving iniquity and transgression, but he will by no means leave the guilty unpunished, visiting the iniquity of the fathers upon the sons, upon the third and upon the fourth generation.'

**19** Pardon, I pray, the iniquity of this people according to the greatness of your steadfast love, and just as you have forgiven this people from Egypt even until now."

---

### YHWH's Judgment: Pardon and Sentence (vv. 20-25)

**20** And YHWH said, "I have pardoned according to your word.

**21** But as I live, and as all the earth shall be filled with the glory of YHWH,

**22** surely all the men who have seen my glory and my signs which I performed in Egypt and in the wilderness, and have tested me these ten times and have not listened to my voice,

**23** they shall not see the land which I swore to their fathers. And none of those who despised me shall see it.

**24** But my servant Caleb, because there was another spirit with him and he followed fully after me, I will bring him into the land into which he went, and his seed shall possess it.

**25** Now the Amalekite and the Canaanite dwell in the valley. Tomorrow, turn and set out for the wilderness by the way of the Sea of Reeds."

---

### The Sentence of Forty Years (vv. 26-35)

**26** And YHWH spoke to Moses and to Aaron, saying,

**27** "How long shall I bear with this evil congregation who murmur against me? The murmurings of the Sons of Israel, which they murmur against me, I have heard.

**28** Say to them, 'As I live — declares YHWH — just as you have spoken in my ears, so I will do to you.

**29** In this wilderness your carcasses shall fall, even all your numbered ones according to all your numbering, from twenty years old and upward, who have murmured against me.

**30** You shall surely not come into the land in which I lifted my hand to settle you, except Caleb son of Jephunneh and Joshua son of Nun.

**31** But your little ones, whom you said would become plunder, I will bring them in, and they shall know the land which you have rejected.

**32** But as for you, your carcasses shall fall in this wilderness.

**33** And your sons shall be shepherds in the wilderness forty years, and they shall bear your harlotries until your carcasses are consumed in the wilderness.

**34** According to the number of the days in which you spied out the land, forty days, a day for a year, a day for a year, you shall bear your iniquities forty years, and you shall know my opposition.

**35** I, YHWH, have spoken. Surely this I will do to all this evil congregation who have gathered together against me. In this wilderness they shall be consumed, and there they shall die.'"

---

### Death of the Faithless Spies (vv. 36-38)

**36** And the men whom Moses had sent to spy out the land, and who returned and made the whole congregation murmur against him by bringing out an evil report against the land —

**37** the men who brought out an evil report of the land died by the plague before YHWH.

**38** But Joshua son of Nun and Caleb son of Jephunneh remained alive, from among those men who had gone to spy out the land.

---

### The Presumptuous Assault (vv. 39-45)

**39** And Moses spoke these words to all the Sons of Israel, and the people mourned greatly.

**40** And they rose early in the morning and went up to the top of the mountain, saying, "Here we are, and we will go up to the place which YHWH has spoken of, for we have sinned."

**41** And Moses said, "Why now do you transgress the mouth of YHWH? For it will not succeed.

**42** Do not go up, for YHWH is not in your midst, lest you be struck down before your enemies.

**43** For the Amalekite and the Canaanite are there before you, and you shall fall by the sword. Because you have turned back from following YHWH, YHWH will not be with you."

**44** But they presumed to go up to the top of the mountain. And the ark of the covenant of YHWH and Moses did not depart from the midst of the camp.

**45** And the Amalekite and the Canaanite who dwelt in that hill country came down and struck them and beat them down as far as Hormah.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Textual Notes

1. **Verse 1 -- "And the whole congregation lifted up their voice" (וַתִּשָּׂא כׇּל־הָעֵדָה וַיִּתְּנוּ אֶת־קוֹלָם):** The chapter opens with the collective response to the evil report of 13:32-33. The verb nasa (lifted up) is followed by vayyittenu et qolam (and they gave their voice), a hendiadys meaning they raised a loud cry. The weeping "that night" (balaylah hahu) indicates the immediate aftermath of the spies' report. The LXX (Vaticanus pa_0122) renders και αναλαβουσα πασα η συναγωγη εδωκεν φωνην και εκλαιεν ο λαος ολην την νυκτα εκεινην (and the whole congregation lifted up and gave voice, and the people wept all that night). The LXX adds "all" (ολην) to "that night," intensifying the duration. This verse begins the great rebellion narrative that constitutes the theological climax of Israel's wilderness experience.

2. **Verse 2 -- "Would that we had died" (לוּ מַתְנוּ):** The particle lu introduces a contrary-to-fact wish (optative). The verb matnu (we had died) is the Qal perfect of מות. The wish is expressed twice with devastating parallelism: "Would that we had died in the land of Egypt" and "would that we had died in this wilderness." The double wish is tragically ironic: YHWH will grant the second wish in his judgment (vv. 28-29), and their carcasses will indeed fall in this wilderness. The people's complaint inverts the exodus: they wish they had never left Egypt, negating YHWH's greatest act of redemption. The LXX renders ωφελον απεθανομεν (would that we had died), using the imperfect indicative with the particle for unfulfilled wish.

3. **Verse 3 -- "Our wives and our little ones will become plunder" (נָשֵׁינוּ וְטַפֵּנוּ יִהְיוּ לָבַז):** The word tappenu (our little ones) refers to small children; baz means plunder, spoil. The cruel irony is that these very little ones will be the ones who enter the promised land (v. 31), while the adults who claimed to protect them will perish. The verb "bringing" (mevi) in "why is YHWH bringing us" casts YHWH as the agent of their destruction rather than their deliverer -- a theological inversion of the exodus narrative. The proposal to "return to Egypt" (nashuvah Mitsraymah) represents the ultimate apostasy: a deliberate undoing of the exodus.

4. **Verse 4 -- "Let us appoint a head" (נִתְּנָה רֹאשׁ):** The phrase nittenah rosh (let us give/appoint a head) proposes replacing Moses with a leader who will take them back to Egypt. This is insurrection against both Moses and YHWH. The LXX renders δωμεν αρχηγον (let us give a leader). The proposal echoes Nehemiah 9:17, which refers to this very event. The phrase "each man to his brother" (ish el-achiv) indicates the movement spread as grassroots consensus, not organized conspiracy. The verb nashuv (let us return) to Egypt directly contradicts YHWH's purpose in the exodus. The Leningrad Codex text is clear here on page 164, right column.

5. **Verse 5 -- "And Moses and Aaron fell on their faces" (וַיִּפֹּל מֹשֶׁה וְאַהֲרֹן עַל־פְּנֵיהֶם):** Falling on the face is an act of desperate supplication, grief, and intercession (cf. Numbers 16:4, 22; 20:6). It may also express horror at the people's blasphemy. The LXX renders και επεσεν Μωυσης και Ααρων επι προσωπον (and Moses and Aaron fell upon their face). The phrase "before all the assembly of the congregation" (lifnei kol qehal adat) combines two terms for the gathered people -- qahal (assembly, convocation) and edah (congregation, community) -- emphasizing the full public nature of the crisis.

6. **Verse 6 -- "tore their garments" (קָרְעוּ בִּגְדֵיהֶם):** The rending of garments (qeri'at begadim) is the conventional Israelite expression of grief, horror, or protest (cf. Genesis 37:34; Joshua 7:6; 2 Samuel 1:11). Joshua and Caleb's gesture communicates both mourning over the people's faithlessness and alarm at the coming consequences. The pairing of Joshua (from Ephraim) and Caleb (from Judah) as the two faithful spies brings together the two tribes that will later lead the northern and southern kingdoms respectively.

7. **Verse 7 -- "exceedingly good, very much so" (טוֹבָה הָאָרֶץ מְאֹד מְאֹד):** The double intensifier me'od me'od (very very, exceedingly) is a superlative construction expressing the highest degree. The land is not merely good but surpassingly, abundantly good. The LXX renders αγαθη η γη σφοδρα σφοδρα (the land is good, exceedingly exceedingly). This emphatic assessment directly contradicts the evil report of 13:32 that the land "devours its inhabitants." Caleb and Joshua address the same evidence but draw the opposite conclusion because they evaluate the land through the lens of YHWH's promise rather than human fear.

8. **Verse 9 -- "they are our bread" (כִּי לַחְמֵנוּ הֵם):** The metaphor lachmenu hem (they are our bread/food) means the inhabitants will be easily consumed -- devoured by Israel as one devours bread. This boldly reverses the ten spies' claim that the land "devours its inhabitants" (13:32): it is not the land that devours, but Israel that will consume. The phrase "their shade has departed from upon them" (סָר צִלָּם מֵעֲלֵיהֶם) uses tsillam (their shade, their shadow, their protection). In the ancient Near East, shade/shadow represents divine protection and shelter (cf. Psalm 91:1; 121:5; Isaiah 30:2-3). The departure of the protective shade means the Canaanites' gods have abandoned them; they are defenseless. The LXX renders αφεστηκεν η σκεπη απ αυτων (their protection has departed from them), interpreting tsillam as "protection" rather than literally "shadow." This verse contains the theological core of Joshua and Caleb's counter-report: YHWH's presence with Israel and the withdrawal of divine protection from Canaan combine to make victory certain.

9. **Verse 10 -- "And the whole congregation said to stone them" (וַיֹּאמְרוּ כׇּל־הָעֵדָה לִרְגּוֹם אֹתָם בָּאֲבָנִים):** The response to Joshua and Caleb's faithful testimony is the threat of execution by stoning (regimah). Stoning is the prescribed penalty for blasphemy and covenant violation (Leviticus 24:14; Numbers 15:35-36) -- the irony being that the people attempt to execute the faithful for faithfulness while they themselves are committing the true blasphemy. The sudden appearance of the "glory of YHWH" (kevod YHWH) at the tent of meeting intervenes before the stoning can be carried out. The glory appearance is a theophany of judgment (cf. Exodus 16:10; Numbers 16:19, 42; 20:6). The LXX renders η δοξα κυριου ωφθη εν νεφελη επι της σκηνης του μαρτυριου (the glory of the Lord appeared in the cloud upon the tent of witness), adding "in the cloud" -- an expansion not in the MT.

10. **Verse 11 -- "How long will this people despise me?" (עַד־אָנָה יְנַאֲצֻנִי הָעָם הַזֶּה):** The verb ni'ets (to despise, to spurn, to treat with contempt) is stronger than mere disobedience; it denotes active contempt for YHWH's character and promises. The parallel question "how long will they not believe in me" (ad-anah lo ya'aminu bi) identifies the root sin as unbelief, not merely fear. The phrase "with all the signs" (bekhol ha-otot) refers to the plagues, the sea crossing, the manna, the water from the rock -- the cumulative evidence of YHWH's power and faithfulness that should have produced trust. The LXX renders εως τινος παροξυνει με ο λαος ουτος (how long will this people provoke me), using παροξυνω (to provoke) for the Hebrew ni'ets.

11. **Verse 12 -- "I will strike them with pestilence and dispossess them" (אַכֶּנּוּ בַדֶּבֶר וְאוֹרִשֶׁנּוּ):** The twin verbs nakah (to strike) and yarash (Hiphil: to dispossess, to disinherit) threaten both destruction and disinheritance. The dever (pestilence, plague) is a standard instrument of divine judgment (cf. Exodus 9:3, 15). The offer to make Moses into "a nation greater and mightier" (legoy gadol ve-atsum) echoes the promise to Abraham (Genesis 12:2) and the identical offer made at the golden calf incident (Exodus 32:10). Moses refuses the offer both times, interceding for the people. The parallel between the golden calf and the spy rebellion is deliberate: both are crises of faith that nearly result in national annihilation, and both are resolved through Moses' intercession.

12. **Verse 13-16 -- Moses' Argument from YHWH's Reputation:** Moses' intercession is structured as a legal argument with two premises: (a) the Egyptians and the nations have heard of YHWH's mighty deeds (vv. 13-14), and (b) if YHWH destroys Israel, the nations will conclude that YHWH was unable to fulfill his promise (vv. 15-16). The argument appeals not to Israel's merit but to YHWH's own honor among the nations. The phrase "eye to eye" (ayin be-ayin) in verse 14 means YHWH is seen directly, face to face, by his people -- a remarkable claim of divine intimacy. The pillar of cloud and fire (v. 14) are the visible manifestation of YHWH's guiding presence. The LXX follows the MT closely through this section. Moses' argument essentially says: Your name is at stake; Your reputation among the nations depends on Israel's survival. This is not manipulation but theology -- YHWH's purpose for Israel includes being known among the nations as the God who keeps his covenant.

13. **Verse 17 -- "let the power of my Lord be great" (יִגְדַּל־נָא כֹּחַ אֲדֹנָי):** Having established that YHWH's reputation is at stake, Moses now appeals to YHWH's own self-revelation. The "power" (koach) of YHWH is demonstrated not by destroying the sinful people (which any deity might do) but by exercising the attributes Moses is about to quote: patience, love, and forgiveness balanced with justice. True divine power is shown in restraint.

14. **Verse 18 -- "YHWH is slow to anger and great in steadfast love" (יְהוָה אֶרֶךְ אַפַּיִם וְרַב־חֶסֶד):** Moses quotes from the divine self-revelation at Sinai (Exodus 34:6-7), the foundational text of YHWH's character. The phrase erekh appayim (long of nostrils, i.e., slow to anger) is an anthropomorphic idiom: the nostrils flare in anger, so "long nostrils" means anger is slow to rise. The word chesed (steadfast love, covenant loyalty, faithful kindness) is the central covenantal virtue, denoting YHWH's committed faithfulness to his promises. The phrase "forgiving iniquity and transgression" (nose avon vafesha) uses nasa (to bear, to carry, to lift) -- YHWH bears the weight of sin away. But the balancing clause -- "he will by no means leave the guilty unpunished" (venaqeh lo yenaqqeh) -- uses the infinitive absolute for emphasis: "acquitting, he will not acquit." Justice and mercy are held in tension: sin is forgiven but not ignored. The visitation of iniquity upon subsequent generations (al-banim al-shilleshim ve-al-ribbe'im) reflects the corporate solidarity of the Israelite family unit and the long-term consequences of covenant unfaithfulness, not arbitrary punishment of the innocent (cf. Deuteronomy 24:16; Ezekiel 18:20 for the complementary individual principle).

15. **Verse 19 -- "Pardon, I pray, the iniquity of this people" (סְלַח־נָא לַעֲוֺן הָעָם הַזֶּה):** The verb salach (to pardon, to forgive) is used exclusively of divine forgiveness in the Hebrew Bible -- humans do not salach; only God does. The appeal is "according to the greatness of your steadfast love" (kegodel chasdekha) -- forgiveness is grounded in YHWH's character, not Israel's repentance. The phrase "from Egypt even until now" (me-Mitsrayim ve-ad hennah) acknowledges a pattern of forgiveness: YHWH has repeatedly pardoned this people from the exodus onward, and Moses asks for continuity with that pattern. The LXX renders αφες την αμαρτιαν τω λαω τουτω (forgive the sin of this people).

16. **Verse 20 -- "I have pardoned according to your word" (סָלַחְתִּי כִּדְבָרֶךָ):** YHWH's response is terse and immediate: salachthi (I have pardoned). The verb is in the perfect tense, indicating a completed act. The pardon is granted "according to your word" (kidvarekha), acknowledging Moses' intercession as effectual. However, as the following verses make clear, pardon does not mean absence of consequences. Forgiveness removes the threat of annihilation (v. 12) but does not cancel the disciplinary sentence. This distinction between pardon and consequence is foundational to biblical theology: YHWH forgives the nation and sustains the covenant, but the generation that rebelled will not enter the land.

17. **Verse 21 -- "as all the earth shall be filled with the glory of YHWH" (וְיִמָּלֵא כְבוֹד־יְהוָה אֶת־כׇּל־הָאָרֶץ):** The oath formula "as I live" (chay ani) is the strongest possible divine assertion. The vision of the earth filled with YHWH's glory looks beyond the immediate crisis to an eschatological fulfillment (cf. Isaiah 6:3; Habakkuk 2:14). The juxtaposition of pardon (v. 20) with the oath of universal glory (v. 21) places the judgment that follows within the larger frame of YHWH's redemptive purpose for all creation.

18. **Verse 22 -- "and have tested me these ten times" (וַיְנַסּוּ אֹתִי זֶה עֶשֶׂר פְּעָמִים):** The "ten times" (eser pe'amim) may be a round number for "repeatedly" or may refer to ten specific instances of testing. Rabbinic tradition (Mishnah Avot 5:4; Arakhin 15a-b) enumerates ten tests: (1) at the sea (Exodus 14:11), (2) at Marah (Exodus 15:24), (3) in the wilderness of Sin -- no food (Exodus 16:3), (4) manna violation -- leaving till morning (Exodus 16:20), (5) manna violation -- Sabbath gathering (Exodus 16:27), (6) at Rephidim -- no water (Exodus 17:2), (7) the golden calf (Exodus 32), (8) at Taberah (Numbers 11:1), (9) at Kibroth-hattaavah -- craving meat (Numbers 11:4), and (10) the spy rebellion (Numbers 13-14). The LXX renders δεκα πειρασμους (ten temptations/trials).

19. **Verse 24 -- "my servant Caleb" (עַבְדִּי כָלֵב):** The title "my servant" (avdi) is a high distinction in the Hebrew Bible, applied to Abraham (Genesis 26:24), Moses (Numbers 12:7-8), David (2 Samuel 7:5), and the Isaianic servant. Caleb's distinction is explained: "another spirit was with him" (ru'ach acheret immô) and "he followed fully after me" (vayemallé acharay). The phrase ru'ach acheret (a different spirit) contrasts Caleb's inner disposition with the fearful spirit of the ten spies. The verb mille' acharay (literally "he filled after me") means he followed YHWH completely, without reservation. The LXX renders πνευμα ετερον εν αυτω (another spirit in him). Notably, Joshua is not mentioned here alongside Caleb, though he is included in the exception at verse 30. The omission may reflect the tradition that Caleb's faithfulness was voluntary and remarkable, while Joshua's was expected as Moses' attendant.

20. **Verse 25 -- "the Amalekite and the Canaanite dwell in the valley" (וְהָעֲמָלֵקִי וְהַכְּנַעֲנִי יוֹשֵׁב בָּעֵמֶק):** This transitional verse redirects Israel's route. Rather than proceeding northward into Canaan (now barred to this generation), they must turn and head toward the Sea of Reeds (yam suph), back into the wilderness. The mention of the Amalekite and Canaanite in the valley serves as both a tactical warning (do not attempt an assault) and a theological sign (the way into Canaan is blocked by judgment). The "way of the Sea of Reeds" (derekh yam-suph) leads southward and eastward, toward the Gulf of Aqaba, beginning the long wilderness detour. The LXX renders ο Αμαληκ και ο Χαναναιος κατοικουσιν εν τω κοιλωματι (the Amalekite and the Canaanite dwell in the hollow).

21. **Verse 27 -- "How long shall I bear with this evil congregation?" (עַד־מָתַי לָעֵדָה הָרָעָה הַזֹּאת):** YHWH now speaks the formal sentence. The word edah (congregation) is the same term for the covenant community, but qualified by ra'ah (evil). The murmurings (telunnot) are specified as directed "against me" (alay) -- the people's complaint against Moses and Aaron is ultimately against YHWH. The LXX renders εως τινος την συναγωγην την πονηραν ταυτην (how long this evil congregation).

22. **Verse 28 -- "As I live" (חַי־אָנִי):** The second occurrence of the oath formula in this passage (cf. v. 21) introduces the detailed sentence. The phrase "just as you have spoken in my ears" (ka'asher dibbartem be-oznay) turns the people's own words against them. They wished to die in the wilderness (v. 2); YHWH grants their wish. This is the principle of measure-for-measure (middah keneged middah): the punishment corresponds precisely to the sin. The LXX renders ζω εγω λεγει κυριος (as I live, says the Lord), adding "says the Lord" as a prophetic formula not in the MT.

23. **Verse 29 -- "your carcasses shall fall" (בַּמִּדְבָּר הַזֶּה יִפְּלוּ פִגְרֵיכֶם):** The word pigreikhem (your carcasses, your corpses) is a deliberately degrading term (cf. Isaiah 14:19; 34:3; Amos 8:3). Those who are twenty years old and upward -- the military census age (cf. Numbers 1:3) -- and who murmured are sentenced. The "numbering" (misparkhem) refers back to the census of Numbers 1-2. The LXX renders τα κωλα υμων πεσειται (your limbs shall fall), using κωλα (limbs, members) for the Hebrew pigrim (carcasses).

24. **Verse 30 -- "in which I lifted my hand to settle you" (אֲשֶׁר נָשָׂאתִי אֶת־יָדִי לְשַׁכֵּן אֶתְכֶם בָּהּ):** The lifting of the hand (nesiat yad) is the gesture of oath-taking (cf. Genesis 14:22; Deuteronomy 32:40; Ezekiel 20:5-6). The verb shakhen (to settle, to dwell, to tabernacle) is the root from which shekhinah (divine presence/dwelling) derives. YHWH had sworn not merely to give them the land but to settle them in it -- to establish them in secure habitation. The exception of Caleb and Joshua is stated explicitly. The LXX adds "and those registered from twenty years and above" to make the scope explicit.

25. **Verse 31 -- "whom you said would become plunder" (אֲשֶׁר אֲמַרְתֶּם לָבַז יִהְיֶה):** YHWH again quotes the people's own words back to them (cf. v. 3). The children they claimed would become plunder will instead inherit the land. The verb yada (to know) in "they shall know the land" means experiential knowledge -- they will possess, inhabit, and enjoy the land "which you have rejected" (asher me'astem bah). The verb ma'as (to reject, to refuse, to despise) is the same verb used for Saul's rejection of YHWH's word (1 Samuel 15:23, 26). The people have not merely feared the land; they have rejected YHWH's gift.

26. **Verse 33 -- "your sons shall be shepherds in the wilderness forty years" (וּבְנֵיכֶם יִהְיוּ רֹעִים בַּמִּדְבָּר אַרְבָּעִים שָׁנָה):** The verb ro'im (shepherds, pastoral wanderers) describes the nomadic existence of the wilderness period. The children will "bear your harlotries" (venase'u et-zenuteikhem) -- the term zenut (harlotry, unfaithfulness) is the prophetic metaphor for covenant infidelity (cf. Hosea 1-3; Ezekiel 16, 23). The innocent next generation suffers the consequences of their parents' rebellion, bearing the burden of displacement without bearing the guilt. The forty years runs from the exodus, not from this point; roughly thirty-eight years remain (cf. Deuteronomy 2:14).

27. **Verse 34 -- "a day for a year, a day for a year" (יוֹם לַשָּׁנָה יוֹם לַשָּׁנָה):** The repeated phrase yom lashanah yom lashanah (a day for a year, a day for a year) establishes the calculus of judgment: forty days of spying equals forty years of wandering. The phrase "you shall know my opposition" (vidatem et-tenu'ati) uses the rare word tenu'ah, which occurs only here in the Hebrew Bible. Its meaning is debated: "opposition," "withdrawal," "alienation," or "frustration of purpose." The LXX renders γνωσεσθε τον θυμον της οργης μου (you shall know the fury of my wrath). The Vulgate renders ultionem meam (my vengeance). The word likely conveys YHWH's active turning against those who turned against him -- a measured, deliberate withdrawal of favor.

28. **Verse 35 -- "I, YHWH, have spoken" (אֲנִי יְהוָה דִּבַּרְתִּי):** The self-identification formula seals the oracle with the full weight of divine authority. The phrase "evil congregation" (edah hara'ah) frames the sentence (cf. v. 27). The verbs "be consumed" (yittammu) and "die" (yamutu) reinforce the finality: the present generation will not survive the wilderness period. The LXX renders εγω κυριος ελαλησα (I the Lord have spoken).

29. **Verse 36-37 -- Death of the Ten Spies:** The immediate execution of the ten faithless spies by plague (maggephah) before YHWH serves as the first installment of the judgment pronounced in verses 26-35. The word maggephah (plague, striking, smiting) indicates a sudden, supernatural death, not a natural illness. The death of the ten is distinct from and prior to the forty-year sentence upon the general population. Their crime is specified: they "brought out an evil report against the land" (motsi'ei dibbat ha-arets ra'ah) -- the same phrase from 13:32. The LXX renders απεθανον εν τη πληγη εναντι κυριου (they died in the plague before the Lord).

30. **Verse 38 -- "Joshua son of Nun and Caleb son of Jephunneh remained alive" (וִיהוֹשֻׁעַ בִּן־נוּן וְכָלֵב בֶּן־יְפֻנֶּה חָיוּ):** The verb chayu (they lived, they remained alive) stands in stark contrast to the death by plague of their ten colleagues. Their survival is both a reward for faithfulness and a sign of hope: the two who declared "we are well able" (13:30) live to prove it. The order here is Joshua first, then Caleb -- reversing the order of 13:30 where Caleb spoke first. Joshua's primacy here may anticipate his future role as Moses' successor and the leader of the conquest.

31. **Verse 40 -- "Here we are, and we will go up" (הִנֶּנּוּ וְעָלִינוּ):** The interjection hinnenu (here we are, behold us) echoes Abraham's hinneni (Genesis 22:1) and Isaiah's hinneni (Isaiah 6:8), but without the obedient spirit those passages convey. The people now express willingness to attack -- too late. Their confession "for we have sinned" (ki chatanu) is genuine recognition of sin but not genuine repentance, because repentance would involve obedience to YHWH's new command (to turn southward, v. 25), not a unilateral attempt to fulfill the old command (to go up into the land). The LXX renders ιδου ημεις αναβησομεθα (behold, we will go up).

32. **Verse 41 -- "Why now do you transgress the mouth of YHWH?" (לָמָּה זֶּה אַתֶּם עֹבְרִים אֶת־פִּי יְהוָה):** The verb overim (transgressing, crossing over) with pi YHWH (the mouth of YHWH) means violating YHWH's spoken command. Moses' warning "it will not succeed" (vehi lo titsalach) uses the verb tsalach (to prosper, to succeed). Military success depends on YHWH's presence and favor, which have now been explicitly withdrawn. The people who refused to go up when commanded now insist on going up when forbidden -- a pattern of perverse disobedience that contradicts YHWH at every turn.

33. **Verse 43 -- "Because you have turned back from following YHWH" (כִּי עַל־כֵּן שַׁבְתֶּם מֵאַחֲרֵי יְהוָה):** The reason for YHWH's absence is stated with clarity: they "turned back from following YHWH" (shavtem me-acharei YHWH). The verb shuv (to turn, to return) in this context means apostasy -- turning away from following. The clause "YHWH will not be with you" (velo yihyeh YHWH immakhem) is the most devastating statement in the passage: the withdrawal of the divine presence is the withdrawal of all protection, guidance, and power. Without YHWH, Israel is not merely weakened but helpless.

34. **Verse 44 -- "But they presumed to go up" (וַיַּעְפִּלוּ לַעֲלוֹת):** The verb ya'pilu (they presumed, they acted presumptuously) from the root עפל occurs only here and in Habakkuk 2:4 (where it describes the arrogant soul). It conveys reckless audacity, headstrong defiance. The notation that "the ark of the covenant of YHWH and Moses did not depart from the midst of the camp" confirms that neither the symbol of YHWH's presence (the ark) nor YHWH's appointed leader (Moses) accompanied the assault. The people go alone, without divine sanction or human leadership, into inevitable defeat. The LXX renders διαβιασαμενοι ανεβησαν (having forced their way, they went up).

35. **Verse 45 -- "and beat them down as far as Hormah" (וַיַּכּוּם וַיַּכְּתוּם עַד־הַחָרְמָה):** The double verb vayyakkum vayyakketum (and struck them and beat them down) uses two different roots for smiting (נכה and כתת), intensifying the defeat. Hormah (חָרְמָה) is derived from cherem (the ban, total destruction), making the place-name bitterly ironic: the Israelites, who should have been the agents of cherem against the Canaanites, are themselves destroyed at the place bearing that name. The location of Hormah is debated but generally placed in the northern Negev, near Arad (cf. Numbers 21:1-3; Judges 1:17). The LXX renders εως Ερμαν (unto Herman). This defeat, immediately following the divine sentence, confirms that Israel's military capacity is entirely dependent on YHWH's presence and entirely void without it.

### LXX Divergences

The LXX of Numbers 14 in Codex Vaticanus (pa_0122 to pa_0123) follows the MT more closely than in chapter 13, though several notable divergences exist. (a) In verse 9, the LXX does not include the phrase "they are our bread" but renders the protective metaphor differently. (b) In verse 10, the LXX adds "in the cloud" (εν νεφελη) to the appearance of YHWH's glory, harmonizing with other theophany accounts. (c) In verse 24, the LXX does not mention Joshua alongside Caleb in the exception, matching the MT, but the Vaticanus text shows slight orthographic variations in the proper names. (d) In verse 25, the LXX omits the specific mention of "the way of the Sea of Reeds" in some textual witnesses, though Vaticanus preserves it. (e) In verse 34, the LXX renders the rare Hebrew tenu'ati as "the fury of my wrath" (τον θυμον της οργης μου), providing an interpretive expansion of a term whose meaning was apparently already uncertain to the translators. (f) Throughout the chapter, the Vaticanus manuscript shows the typical features of the codex: continuous uncial script without word division, occasional nomina sacra (ΚΣ for κυριος), and marginal chapter markings. The transition from chapter 13 to 14 on pa_0122 is marked by the section number ΛΣ in the left margin. The overall narrative and theological content is consistent between the MT and LXX, with the Septuagint occasionally expanding or clarifying where the Hebrew is terse or ambiguous.

### Structural Observations

- Numbers 14 is the theological pivot of the entire book of Numbers and one of the most consequential chapters in the Pentateuch. The chapter falls into five movements: the people's rebellion (vv. 1-4), the faithful response of Moses, Aaron, Joshua, and Caleb (vv. 5-10a), the divine theophany and dialogue between YHWH and Moses (vv. 10b-25), the formal sentence upon the congregation (vv. 26-38), and the failed invasion demonstrating the irrevocability of the sentence (vv. 39-45). Each movement escalates the tension: from weeping to insurrection, from intercession to judgment, from sentence to catastrophe.

- The chapter is structured around three speeches: the people's speech of rebellion (vv. 2-4), Moses' speech of intercession (vv. 13-19), and YHWH's speech of judgment (vv. 20-35). The people speak to each other; Moses speaks to YHWH; YHWH speaks through Moses. The content of each speech determines its outcome: the people's words bring death upon themselves (v. 28 -- "just as you have spoken in my ears"), Moses' words bring pardon (v. 20), and YHWH's words establish the definitive shape of Israel's future.

- The principle of measure-for-measure (middah keneged middah) governs the entire judgment: they wished to die in the wilderness (v. 2) -- they will die in the wilderness (v. 35); they said their children would become plunder (v. 3) -- their children will inherit the land (v. 31); they tested YHWH ten times (v. 22) -- they will wander one year for each day of the spy mission (v. 34); they refused to go up when commanded (13:31) -- they will be defeated when they presume to go up unbidden (v. 45). The judgment is not arbitrary but precisely calibrated to the sin.

- Moses' intercession (vv. 13-19) is a masterpiece of theological argumentation. He does not appeal to the people's merit (they have none), nor to YHWH's mercy in isolation. Instead, he constructs a three-part argument: (1) YHWH's reputation among the nations requires Israel's survival (vv. 13-16), (2) YHWH's own self-revealed character includes patience and forgiveness (vv. 17-18), and (3) YHWH has an established pattern of forgiving this people (v. 19). The argument moves from the external (what the nations will say) to the internal (who YHWH has declared himself to be) to the historical (what YHWH has already done). It is the most fully developed intercessory prayer in the Pentateuch, exceeding even Moses' prayer at the golden calf (Exodus 32:11-13).

- The distinction between pardon and consequence (vv. 20-23) is theologically fundamental. YHWH pardons (salachthi, v. 20) but does not revoke the sentence (vv. 22-23). The nation survives, the covenant continues, the next generation will enter the land -- but this generation will not. Forgiveness addresses the relational breach (YHWH does not destroy Israel); discipline addresses the behavioral pattern (the faithless generation faces consequences). This paradigm recurs throughout Scripture and is essential to understanding divine justice: forgiveness is not the elimination of consequences but the preservation of relationship through consequences.

- The failed invasion (vv. 39-45) functions as a narrative coda that demonstrates the irrevocability of divine judgment. The people's belated willingness to attack is not repentance but presumption -- an attempt to undo judgment by unilateral action. Moses' warning (vv. 41-43) and the disaster at Hormah (v. 45) together prove that obedience delayed is not obedience at all, and that military action without divine authorization is self-destruction. The ark remains in the camp (v. 44), a visible sign that YHWH is not with the assault force. The chapter thus ends where it began: with the people acting against YHWH's expressed will, and suffering the consequences.

- The contrast between Caleb and the congregation extends the literary architecture of the spy narrative. Caleb has "another spirit" (ru'ach acheret, v. 24); the congregation has the spirit of rebellion. Caleb "followed fully" after YHWH; the congregation "turned back from following" YHWH (v. 43). Caleb's seed will "possess" the land (v. 24); the congregation's carcasses will "fall" in the wilderness (v. 29). The chapter presents faithfulness and rebellion as two complete and mutually exclusive orientations toward YHWH, each with its corresponding destiny.
